Those looking to hire bacoo for mercenary work should visit one of the three gathering spots along the coast. These locations do not appear to be villages because there is no sign that bacoo live there, despite their perpetual presence lounging in tree branches. Scuttle Reef is near one of the islands extending from the Hook. As the name suggests, scuttle bacoos are most common there. They work as guides or scavengers for those looking to recover objects from shipwrecks. Halfway up the coast from Scuttle Reef to Kraken is Mother Mangrove, an enormous mangrove tree that towers over the others and is home to a variety of bacoo. Bacoo here tend to be skilled sailors and are always in high demand by pirate crews. Ships visit and compete to finish tasks set forth by those they're trying to recruit to prove themselves most worthy of the skilled bacoo's services. Finally, Banana Grove is about half a day's walk south of Kraken and is popular with people seeking medical assistance or help researching obscure lore.

Finally, the wreck of Jenna's Love will surely quicken any adventurer's pulse. You might be surprised to learn that the most famous shipwreck in the region is not off the Hook's coastline. Nay, it's miles inland! While pirates often joke that the Shivering Jungle is so swampy you could sail a ship right through it, that's an exaggeration. No one knows how the wreck wound up in its present location, not just grounded but impaled upon a massive dead cypress. It holds tight to its secrets, for the ship is haunted by an aggressive g'mayun crew that attacks intruders on sight. Xotlxotl in the area use it as a proving ground to see how far they can get into the ruin before being forced to retreat. Legend says the ship was transporting a magical artifact so powerful that its guards remained determined to protect their charge even in death. To date, no one has actually reached the cargo hold, although many who have tried and failed are said to have joined the undead crew in guarding its secrets.

Tentacle River.name: 触手之河 Tentacle River
Tentacle River.text:
How many dragons can you fit in the Tentacle River? Zero! Do you think a dragon would let you shove them into a river?

If we are talking sheer size, however, the Tentacle River is deep—many, many dragons deep. No matter how many boardwalks and houseboats people build atop it, they'll never constitute more than dust drifting on the surface. But its depths allow for a near-endless buffet of riches. Anglers catch nearly anything imaginable living under the sea in the Tentacle River. Tales also speak of catching (or having been caught by) massive sea monsters, including megalodons, giant squids, water demons, and creatures so rare as to be unidentifiable. Not every section of the river is a terrifying abyss, of course. It meanders and splits into many offshoots. Some terminate in creeks or lakes, while others bend back into the main artery. Where the river is shallow, some believe it diverges vertically with an underground component that descends deep into the earth.

The climate is temperate across the region and throughout the year, though it becomes drier and cooler farther from the coast. Pockets of instability wrack the river, however, resulting in sudden thunderstorms, thick fog, rough currents, or lightning from almost clear skies. Many attribute divine or magical causes to the turmoil, but the depth of the river explains much of it. Collisions of deep currents, upwelling from hot springs, and movement between layers of fresh and saline water result in chaotic effects on the environment. However, claiming none of the phenomena are supernatural in origin would be a step too far.

People And Culture

A rough and tumble culture persists from the days before Undayo of the Stonegulch wrangled some semblance of order into Kraken—look elsewhere to find out more about the town. Enclaves of bacoo, chochori, and xotlxotl are common along the river. Some find their gruffness off-putting, but a direct attitude is a good sign—river folk don't act polite unless they're offended. Word travels fast about who's a troublemaker and which newcomers are making waves. Those who go out of their way to help those in need will find their good behavior rewarded, while those who violate customs will find themselves quickly ostracized.

Fishing is the common interest that binds all ancestries in the region. Tournaments are common along the Tentacle River, and the riverfolk look forward to them enthusiastically, especially since massive feasts always follow them! Those whose catches break the scales often find their room and board covered, while unusual hauls bring in free drinks from locals looking to sate their curiosity.

Cave diving is a dangerous sport that only a cautious few pursue. Discovering a network of caverns, recovering sunken treasure, or identifying a new species that is edible or medicinal can mint a new celebrity overnight, but everyone knows the true experts from the fools looking for a quick score. Those who don't respect the river's strength soon find themselves overpowered by it.

Several festivals throughout the year bring the disparate communities together and make even the surliest sailor crack a smile. Renewal is a summer celebration that coincides with the river's highest levels. Families decorate houseboats to resemble sea creatures, or eldamon, and put on week-long parades, touring neighboring villages to share homemade delicacies. It evolved from Eld traditions looking to placate elemental spirits so that the floods would spare villages but grant bountiful harvests.

Go Than Dat! is a festival that occurs during the winter, with the exact date varying from community to community. During the festival, people in the area spend the day tending to the forests, planting seeds, and clearing underbrush to enable healthy growth in the spring. It ensures the continued availability of the quality tuskwood that builders use for boats and homes while honoring Than Dat's work protecting the natural world. To "go Than Dat" means to become mindful of one's impact on the world and give back to the earth that sustains life.

Salmon Running Month lasts for the duration of the annual salmon migration upriver, which occurs at the end of autumn. Most folks dock their boats to keep the waterways clear for the returning fish and marvel at the density of the run, with the fish sometimes crowding so much that the river looks solid. To pass the time, many make colorful fish kites that they battle in the air. Of course, some fishing takes place from the shore, because who could pass up such a bounty? But villages enforce strict quotas to ensure enough salmon spawn for future years.

Points Of Interest

An experienced guide is absolutely essential for those seeking to navigate the Tentacle River without falling victim to its maze-like twists and capricious conditions. Most guides in Kraken are only good for tourists. They'll take you to some popular fishing spots and then "accidentally" drift upon a collection of houseboats selling souvenirs and fried fish. Still, this is often enough adventure for most, as the Tentacle River has claimed more than its share of ambitious explorers. But if you are daring and wish to truly explore the river, you'll want to visit the village of Tradepost before you begin your journey.

Tradepost

Settlement 1

  • N
  • Village

Chochori trading center.

Government Arbiter (elected official)

Population 82 (43% chochori, 30% g'mayun, 10% kragrak, 9% bacoo, 5% orpok, 3% other)

Languages Chochori, Common

Religions the Balance, the Eld

Threats dangerous magic items, scammers, unpredictable river currents

River Trading Post Tradepost is home to guides and fishers skilled at retrieving goods from the river. Visitors to Tradepost can hire NPCs up to 7th level to navigate the Tentacle River, and items up to 5th level are available for purchase by placing an order.

Floodtamer vergiss chochori architect builder of Tradepost, unofficial leader of Tradepost's chochori population

Markokarn female kragrak arbiter interpreter of contracts, keeper of laws, and leader of Tradepost

N'toyen Fusia female g'mayun authenticator antiques shopkeeper, item identification expert

Shuloo female bacoo advocate contract writer, loophole exploiter

If you follow the Tentacle River a few miles upstream until the boats from Kraken thin out, clear signage will lead you to the half-submerged village of Tradepost, one of the more interesting minor settlements in the vicinity that you should consider visiting (and, importantly, one of the few settlements on the island where your beloved author and guide aren't banned for life). The outpost bears such an unremarkable name for a simple reason: it started as little more than a meeting ground for river chochori to offer goods and services to residents of the city of Kraken downstream. However, in a tale as old as time, business proved so popular that it evolved into a permanent settlement, now housing residents of multiple ancestries, in order to facilitate the increasing burden of trade.

Tradepost is technically two villages combined into one. Chochori residents tend to keep to themselves in coral structures underwater. Their leader is a renowned builder who devised a complex series of living coral locks that channel water in different directions during floods, protecting established towns from disaster without disrupting the river's ecosystem. For reasons never fully explained, Floodtamer moved on from such a signature achievement to focus on building up Tradepost. The reclusive architect rarely makes public pronouncements, giving them great weight when they occur.

About half the structures on the land are Floodtamer constructions. They extend into the river and consist of coral networks that pump water to keep them and their occupants hydrated. The remaining buildings are g'mayun in design and built from wood. The exception is the courthouse, a granite landmark with Tradepost's legal code carved into its walls. The leader elected to enforce the village's laws and arbitrate contract disputes there. Unofficially, everyone knows Floodtamer can overrule Arbiter Markokarn, but Floodtamer doesn't get involved in day-to-day business.

The laws in this outpost focus on facilitating fair trade and preventing violence. Every legal merchant should have a license, so make sure you ask to see one before handing over payment! However, the number of people passing through means scammers occasionally slip in. The arbiter cracks down quickly when she hears of such crimes, and her justice is harsh. Tradepost permanently bans those who break its laws and sends arrested criminals to Kraken for further prosecution if needed. Otherwise, they use a simpler solution: they throw the criminals into the currents with a makeshift raft to fend for themselves—river justice.

Tradepost is an excellent place to gear up for expeditions up the Tentacle River. Merchants sell all manner of adventuring gear. Gamblers can try their luck buying seemingly worthless junk pulled from the river and testing which items are magic items! Many authenticators live in town and are available for hire to identify items, verify the validity of licenses, and bear witness to contracts. I personally recommend N'toyen Fusia for these needs and more. Plus, you can fill the time while she works by browsing restored antiques in her shop, Bird's Eye Renew.

Adventurers sometimes also need advocates to help them sort out the details of their contracts. Some parties want a license to sell goods they recovered; they could sell to existing shops but at lower prices than they can command with their own license. Others choose to hire mercenaries with the promise to share a percentage of their loot. They can also pay a premium for dealers to keep an eye out for specific items while they're exploring or place an order for a crafted item to be ready when they return.

Any guide with a valid license is reputable enough, but the best requires effort to hire. Keeper of Secrets (terath chochori guide) is eccentric and only works for adventurers who impress him. Finding him is the first test. Investigate for signs of wildlife activity, either asking if there have been incidents in the surrounding area or searching for tracks. Keeper may be in the river or the jungle, but he's always found near the village. He isn't a hunter, instead trying to drive away dangerous beasts so no deadly confrontations occur at all. This information is well known among residents of the outpost, and any adventurer who helps him on his mission can gain his aid in return. Keeper knows significantly more about the river than anyone else and can be invaluable as a guide if you can find him and then impress him enough that he deems you worth his time!

Boggard Row

As you continue down the river, and once Mount Auk looms overhead, boggards become common along the southern shore. Their settlements are deeper in the Shivering Jungle, but they hide where trees encroach over the river and ambush unwitting travelers with their tongues. Such attacks might have been mistaken for tentacles in the past, leading to one of the legends about how the river earned its name. However, with so much strange fauna swimming around, I imagine something more impressive than boggards was the true source of the epithet.

Even so, one tributary informally named Boggard Row is thick with the froglike creatures, but if you brave the assaults long enough, the little croakers vanish like roaches under sunlight. Where they disappear, life-size stone statues of xotlxotl pop up at regular intervals, about one every thousand feet, alternating across the banks. No one knows who put them there, but their presence makes even creatures who physically can't get goosebumps feel like they have them.

There are a few strange details about these statues along Boggard Row. First and foremost, no moss or vines grow on them, as though someone is clearing them off on a frequent and regular basis, despite there being no known settlements nearby. Second, their proportions aren't quite right to represent xotlxotl as we know them, with the limbs or the body of each statue seeming too long, and what's more, those who have seen them swear that the number of gill stalks on each statue changes each time you try to count them. Lastly, and most unnervingly of all, there's the hissing, like a snake is coiled on your shoulder, but whenever you turn, the hissing continues to be just beyond your perception. No one knows whether the hissing comes from the statues, whoever is removing the moss, or something else.

Locals say that if you touch one of the statues, boggards leave you alone for months afterward, but you'll have strange dreams of towering beasts that resemble xotlxotl descriptions of their First Gods. They often ignore you, continuing with their incompressible machinations, but sometimes they notice you and make direct eye contact, causing you to experience nightmares beyond description. And when you wake, your body is covered with claw marks. Hence, I suggest you leave the statues alone.

Dive Pads

Those looking to explore underwater will want to watch for blossoming giant lilies. The lilybee is a giant bee about two feet long with blue coloration instead of yellow. It feeds exclusively on the nectar of those water lilies, but periodic flooding can submerge the flowers for weeks or months.

That's why water is no buzzkill for these bees. Their honey contains special enzymes that allow creatures to temporarily breathe underwater! One tablespoon of lilybee honey allows a creature to breathe underwater for about an hour. The dosage is very important. Too little, and the effect wears off early. Take too much, and your lungs might explode, causing you to begin suffocating. Don't look at me like that—I didn't make up the rules. Still, even with the risks, lilybee honey is quite popular for underwater explorers as it's fairly plentiful, and with careful practice, one can determine the correct dosage. However, lilybee honey is notoriously unstable and becomes useless if not consumed within a day of extraction from a hive. Magic that preserves food can extend the duration up to two days but not beyond that, so it's best to harvest it fresh near an intended dive site. Anyone selling bottled lilybee honey is most likely a scammer.

The Stillness

Finally, my faithful reader, let me leave you with one of the strangest and most disturbing occurrences on the Tentacle. Sometimes a stillness falls upon the river when particularly thick fog gathers. The water becomes smooth as a mirror. The sound dies away, leaving your ears ringing for lack of stimulation. No amount of paddling can escape the calm, and moisture seeps into your body like dread. Does the fog bring stillness, or does the stillness create fog?

most unsettling experience, like we were in another realm. Our timepieces told us our stranding lasted only fifteen minutes, but it felt like hours. We didn't witness anything strange during this time, but others have said the inky black water below is not an artifact of the fog but the pupil of an immense eye… Credible reports also exist that, when the stillness left, boats found themselves miles away from where they encountered it. What causes this stillness has never been explained, but it's best to remain calm and ride out the experience if you find yourself in this situation.

Prominent Goldcrop Settlements.name: 著名的金穗聚居地 Prominent Goldcrop Settlements
Prominent Goldcrop Settlements.text:
如你所见，我对金穗地的了解可谓既百科全书般全面又洞见十足；我最渴望的莫过于揭开我们岛屿上每一处聚居地更深层的秘密、隐藏的真相，以及那些丑陋的不安——包括他们的政客、英雄与罪犯。然而，唉，除了被每一处聚居地终生封禁之外，一旦我把这些真相抖出来并公之于众（在诽谤与中伤的指控之后，我被建议改称其为“个人观点”），我本人就会遭到可信的威胁：无论是法律层面还是法外手段。为了你，我当然愿意冒着这些不公把它们分享给你——若不是还有一桩更强效的惨剧。你看，可悲的是，这些心胸狭隘的限制，使我无法在缺席的情况下维持对每个聚居地完全最新的信息。而给你任何不够完整的内幕，都将比我被指控的这些荒唐罪名更加令人发指。

因此，我不得不收录一篇关于金穗地最伟大、最大、也最爱诉讼的城市——朗普兰克——的指南，由我亲近的朋友兼熟人扎娜娅撰写。扎娜娅是唯一能让她的指南保持足够最新、值得一读的人；其他指南作者掌握的信息陈旧得可怕，等他们出版时，内容早已毫无用处。

那么其余的聚居地呢？唯一的选择就是雇一位当地向导，亲自带你走一遭。德马蒂·提西玛远没有他们自称的那般老练，但他们那种“见识不浅、爱冒险的虚张声势、再加上笨拙的手忙脚乱”的独特组合，看起来倒也可爱；因此，我建议你让他们带你四处逛逛。

As you can see, my knowledge of Goldcrop is as encyclopedic and insightful as they come, and I would love nothing more than to reveal the deeper secrets, hidden truths, and ugly insecurities of each and every one of our island's settlements, including their politicians, heroes, and criminals. But alas, in addition to being banned for life from each and every one, I've had credible threats placed upon my person, both legal and extralegal, were I to spill the beans and deliver these truths (which, after accusations of slander and libel, I am advised to call "personal opinions" instead). For you, of course, I might brave these injustices and share them with you anyway, were it not for a greater travesty. You see, tragically, these close-minded restrictions have limited my ability to maintain fully up-to-date information about each settlement in absentia. And to give you anything less than the full scoop would be a far more damning crime than these spurious charges of which I am accused.

For this reason, I'm forced to include a guide to Goldcrop's greatest, largest, and most litigious city, Rumplank, written by Zanaya, a close friend and acquaintance. Zanaya is the only one who keeps her guide sufficiently up-to-date for it to be worth reading; other guide authors work with such stale information that by the time they've published, it's already useless.

So where does that leave you for the other settlements? The only option is to hire a local guide to take you through them in person. D'marti Tixima isn't nearly as experienced as they like to claim, but their unique combination of significant knowledge, adventurous bluster, and clumsy flailing can be adorable to watch, so I recommend you have them show you around.

Rumplank.name: 拉姆普兰克 Rumplank
Rumplank.text:
这里是扎娜娅，你可以把我当作各类话题的本地专家。我大半辈子都住在朗普兰克，并以熟知每一条街道、每一家店面、每一条后巷而自豪，就像它们是我的家一样。如果事情发生在朗普兰克，我就知道！过去几个月里，我一直在整理这本朗普兰克指南，来帮助大家更多了解我们这座伟大的城市。让我把你会遇到的人与地方都告诉你。

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-journals.JournalEntry.pb3IJrg1AZwdK0Gm.JournalEntryPage.TeYdBgGQlTemmuhA]{扎娜娅的朗普兰克指南}

Zanaya here, and you can call me a local expert on all sorts of topics. I've lived most of my life in Rumplank, and I pride myself on knowing every street, storefront, and back alley, like they were my home. If it happened in Rumplank, I know about it! I've been putting together this guide to Rumplank over the past few months to help folks learn more about our great city. Let me tell you all about the people and places you'll find.

@UUID[Compendium.battlezoo-world-of-indigo-isles-pf2e.indigo-isles-journals.JournalEntry.pb3IJrg1AZwdK0Gm.JournalEntryPage.TeYdBgGQlTemmuhA]{Zanaya's Guide to Rumplank}

Kraken: The River Jewel.name: 海妖：河之宝石 Kraken: The River Jewel
Kraken: The River Jewel.text:
Kraken

Settlement 7

  • N
  • City

Industrial port city full of riches from the sea.

Government Mercantile Council

Population 8,723 (29% hardriggans, 23% kragraks, 17% g'mayun, 11% xotlxotl, 9% orpoks, 4% chochori, 3% sprites (especially galtzagorris), 1% aeternal dragons, 3% others)

Languages Aquan, Auran, Common, Draconic, Orpok, Sylvan, Terran

Religions the Balance, the Eld

Threats sea monsters, rogue waves, river pirates

The River's Mysterious Bounty Strange and unusual items sometimes find their way from the Tentacle River and into Kraken's markets. Hearing about this month's latest find typically requires a simple Gather Information check, which is enough to determine where to purchase a single item with a level of 2d4+6. At the GM's discretion, the item on sale might even be an uncommon or rare item.

River Captain Sharrak female hardriggan river fishing-ship captain and possible pirate

Sea Captain Ozmis female g'mayun naval pirate hunter and outgoing collector of travel stories

Port Captain Zargek male kragrak shipyard captain and boss of Kraken's secretive tuskwood lumbering interests

River Captain Lura male xotlxotl leader of the Xotlxotl Buccaneers, a group of friendly treasure hunters along the Kraken

Welcome newcomers to D'marti Tixima tours! Today we are to explore my favorite topic, the jewels of the Iidigo Isles, the towns of Goldcop! Don't worry, D'marti is welcome everywhere, which means you will be as well! Let's start where most people begin their journey: the city of Kraken.

When newcomers arrive at the Indigo Isles, their first sight, more often than not, is the bustling harbor of Kraken. From a distance, the ships and buildings of Kraken resemble a g'mayun's wing, with ruby reds and emerald greens sparkling from every hull and rooftop. A forest of masts crowds the piers, ranging from the towering mainmasts of merchant ships to the laundry-draped mizzens of houseboats. Behind the harbor, a boardwalk of shining white wood connects every pier and building of note.

Kraken, the center of commerce for the Indigo Isles, boasts impressive dockyards and a fleet of vessels that trade with the lands to the east. Apart from the bright colors, the city probably looks a lot like the ports you're used to elsewhere. But here's an insider secret for you: much of Kraken's wealth flows not from the sea but from the mysterious Tentacle River that bisects the city and provides its harbor.

Anyone here interested in history? If not, you're on the right island. Here on Goldcrop, we joke that the fields are full of out-of-work historians. We simply don't know much about Kraken's history. What we do know I can relate to you in a few sentences. For hundreds of years before the city of Kraken was chartered, a tiny, raucous settlement thrived here at the mouth of the Tentacle. Back then, Kraken was a floating village of sailors, mostly g'mayuns, kragraks, and xotlxotl, who braved the river's dangers in order to haul up nets full of fish and other treasures. All that changed nearly 250 years ago when Undayo of Stonegulch, one of Poppy's lieutenants, visited the community for the first time. Undayo immediately saw the town's potential. With Kraken's deep harbor, its plentiful food and lumber, and its population of hard-bitten sailors, Undayo imagined a port city that could rival any in the Isles.

Bringing with him an influx of population from his ships' crews, Undayo formed Kraken's first mercantile council, a governing body composed of the most trusted captains under his command, including a number of hardriggan subordinates, along with some of the most important ship captains of the original settlement. To ensure the city took root on solid ground, the council passed laws requiring that businesses be built in permanent buildings on land—a departure from the settlement's original half-shipboard nature. To this day, these laws are (mostly) enforced.

Even today, Kraken has never forgotten its offshore origins. Like an indecisive bather, the city stands with one foot on land and one in the water. Most of its buildings are built within a stone's throw of a seashore or river bank, and while its markets, shipyards, warehouses, bars, and restaurants are rooted on land, most of its population lives on the water, in houseboats or as crew aboard their fishing and merchant vessels.

Life in Kraken

The Tentacle River splits Kraken in two. The more populated district, the south shore, is home to the city's markets, taverns, and inns, as well as the houses of those residents that choose to live ashore. Kraken's trade vessels and houseboat communities also cling to the south shore.

Despite its size and wealth, South Kraken has never abandoned the free-wheeling attitude of its first inhabitants. Along the boardwalk, days are bustling and nights are riotous. By daylight, Kraken's south shore looks much like that of any port city. Rowboats and barges pole their way between larger ships. Sailors stumble in and out of taverns. Boardwalk merchants hawk their goods. When night falls, bonfires spring up along the south beach—and even aboard the occasional ship, even though fires aboard ships are illegal in Kraken. Most nights, you'll find fisherfolk and traders gathering at firesides to swap tall tales about the day's exploits.

While ocean sailors everywhere have their share of hair-raising exploits, in Kraken, the river fishers do the greater share of the storytelling, spinning improbable-sounding yarns about the rocky reefs that migrate up and down the river, the strange fins or eye stalks that appear out of nowhere, the sudden whirlpools, and the glints of false gold at the bottom of deep, clear pools. As one who has seen strange sights myself, let me tell you this: many of their stories have a grain of truth.

In contrast to the south, Kraken's north shore is all business. Many of Kraken's residents have never set foot there, and even one as well-traveled as I—not caring much for manual labor—avoids it whenever I can.

The north shore is the home of Kraken's dockyards, lumbermills, and other industries. It's the only place in the world where people harvest and mill the big tuskwood trees. Ah, tuskwood! Tough as stone, supple as spruce! Shipbuilders swear by it, and its ivory whiteness makes an ideal canvas for bright paints and dyes. Nothing but the old hundred-foot-tall trees will do for the masts and keels of Kraken's ships, and underneath all the brightly painted buildings of Kraken, you'll find tuskwood. (If you want to see the wood in its unpainted beauty, simply take a look at the boardwalk.)

The north shore is nothing but hammering and sawdust by day, and it's practically deserted at night—except for the well-armed private guards that patrol its warehouses. Although you're too wise to need the warning, I always tell people this: don't get any funny ideas about poking around there because those private guards don't usually bother to bring trespassers up on charges. Most of the time, trespassers just don't come back.

People of Kraken

Half of the folk in Kraken work aboard ships, either sailing the Tentacle, out at sea, or building ships in the ‘yards. The other half feeds, clothes, entertains, or guides the first half.

While fantastic fishing is to be found in the Kraken, you need an expensive license for that. Those who can't afford it fish in the sea. Along with fishermen, traders are a common sight at sea, and stories about the wealth and beauty of our islands don't keep travelers like you away either. Kraken has a strong navy, so piracy isn't a terribly common livelihood—but there is a certain amount of… opportunism, especially on the river. I myself have fallen afoul of pirates more than once, although, as you can imagine, few dare to attack a ship if they know D'marti Tixima is aboard.

Ashore, Kraken boasts the usual assortment of harbor folk, from dockworkers to tavernkeepers. Where it's exceptional is in the number of house painters. You can always spot a few g'mayuns with brushes halfway up a scaffolding or hanging off the side of a houseboat, touching up the bright colors that make Kraken the jewel of the river.

Mercantile Council

The 12-member mercantile council meets in Kraken's only stone building, Seawall. To gain a seat on the council, you must be the captain of a ship or the head of a shipyard. Thus, fishing and commercial interests are well looked after. Kraken's small farming and artisan communities have no real governmental power.

The de facto head of the council is River Captain Sharrak (female hardriggan captain). She leads a five- member river captain voting bloc on the council and prides herself on fighting for river folk's interests above all. Even in the face of increasing piracy, she's resistant to any naval oversight of the river, insisting that river captains can take care of themselves. Indeed, she and her allies take care of themselves very well. Sharrak is one of the wealthiest people in Kraken. Some whisper that Sharrak dabbles in a bit of piracy herself (but not me; I whisper no such thing).

Opposing River Captain Sharrak, at nearly every turn, is Sea Captain Ozmis (female g'mayun captain). The head of the local navy, Ozmis, has stamped down on sea piracy in an effort to increase trade with the mainland. She believes the seas won't be safe until the pirate havens along the Kraken River are dissolved as well.

Ozmis is fascinated by life beyond the Indigo Isles. She speaks a dozen languages badly, and she speaks constantly about her post-retirement travel plans. Any foreigner she meets is sure to be invited to one of the lavish parties at her grand mansion.

Port Captain Zargek (male kragrak administrator), the taciturn boss of the Ironworks shipyard along Kraken's north shore, is an imposing figure—seven feet of stone, with a pangeolil, a rock pangolin eldamon, perched on his shoulder. He votes with either Sharrak or Ozmis, depending on who cuts him a better deal. In exchange for his votes, Zargek imposes stringent rules on the export of tuskwood. Zargek is respected (or feared) by everyone who works on the north side of the river, and no one works there without his approval. In particular, the tuskwood lumber camps to the north of Kraken are off-limits to anyone but Zargek's hand- picked associates.

Xotlxotl Buccaneers

The Xotlxotl Buccaneers sport all of the regalia of pirates—black flags and so on—but they're not as bloodthirsty as they look. Led by River Captain Lura (male xotlxotl captain), they live along the river banks and scoot up and down the river in small schooners. They fish enough to be self-sufficient and spend the rest of their time searching for the mysterious caches of treasure that sometimes turn up in the river. More than the other river groups, the xotlxotl buccaneers are friendly to outsiders. Non-xotlxotl and even newcomers to the Isles are welcome to sign onto their crews. Ironworks

Exploring Kraken

The following is a sample of some of the prominent locations found in Kraken.

Ironworks

The Ironworks, the largest shipyard in Kraken, has the facilities to build five three-masted ships at once (although each is a years-long process). Built of tuskwood, Ironworks-class vessels serve both as merchant ships and as naval frigates that guard the port. Locals believe they're the strongest and most durable ships of their size anywhere. Although they've never proven themselves in a major naval battle, they've acquitted themselves well against pirates. If other cities knew about their excellent sailing qualities, they'd probably be eager to snap up all the Ironworks-made ships they could. Port Captain Zargek, of course, will make sure that never happens. It's said that he'd sink an Ironworks ship to the bottom of the sea rather than let it fall into outsiders' hands.

Seawall

Overlooking Kraken is the fort of Seawall. Ships that enter the harbor must do so under its ballistas and catapults. At the castle's foot, one or more Ironworks ships always lie moored at its docks.

Below sea level are the city's dungeons. Although I haven't seen them firsthand, I'm told they're unpleasant and to be avoided: leaky, with occasional outbreaks of green slime. The very lower levels of the dungeon are underwater. It's said that an escaping prisoner can swim down to a hole that leads to the sea and then to safety. If, by chance, that tidbit ever comes in handy, remember your humble tour guide.

Besides acting as the city's first line of defense and its prison, the Seawall is its center of government. The Mercantile Council meets here, around a stone table affectionately nicknamed "Old Rock", after Undayo of Stonegulch, the Council's founding member.

Tentacle River

As its sailors will be quick to tell you, there's something… eerie… about Tentacle River. The main channel is deeper than it has any right to be, and there are spots where it appears to have no bottom at all. Explorers claim that some of its many winding tributaries change course overnight, although that might merely be confusion caused by the river's mazelike nature. When fisherfolk cast their nets, their catch might include a dented gold ewer or a magic ring. And the river is subject to more fog, whirlpools, and storms than it has any right to.

But the most unsettling stories of the Tentacle River concern its monstrous denizens. Nearly every experienced river fisher can tell a tale of an encounter with some monster more commonly found in the open sea, such as a giant shark or sea serpent. The river's very name comes from the massive tentacles that sometimes break the water's surface, undulating for a few moments before disappearing (sometimes after having snatched a sailor from the deck of a ship).

Many river sailors, while worshiping some god of the Balance or revering the Eld, pay equal tribute to the primordial sea monster said to live in these waters. Despite the stories about ships lost to the monster, these sailors claim that the mysterious creature's protection is responsible for the town's good fortune.

The Boardwalk

The boardwalk, a 20-foot-wide tuskwood pathway that lines the beach and both sides of the river, is Kraken's main street. Nearly every shop, tavern, and place of interest opens its doors onto the boardwalk.

Kraken Tongue connects the north and south sides of the boardwalk. It's a drawbridge across the Kraken River that can be raised to allow sea ships access to the river and vice versa—for an exorbitant toll.

Marketeers along the boardwalk sell fish, fruit, and expensive goods from far-off lands. Grizzled sailors offer dubious treasure maps to "suckers"—the local, affectionate term for strangers to the island. Don't be offended! It's not an insult. It merely refers to a local saying about the Tentacle River. Just as a tentacle has a thousand suckers, the Tentacle has a thousand—ahh— newcomers, that is to say, you. In Kraken, they love newcomers, and they appreciate their generosity toward tour guides.

The Leviathan

If you're looking for a quiet place for a drink, might I urge you to avoid the Leviathan? It's the toughest, rowdiest tavern in Kraken—a place straight out of bygone days. It's frequented by mercenary captains and their soldiers, river captains and their crews, and other colorful but disreputable characters.

The Leviathan regulars have a number of odd traditions, including "standing each other a drink," a traditional hardriggan game. When you offer to stand someone a drink, you both climb up on a table, and then you punch or headbutt your opponent as hard as you can. If the blow fails to knock them off the table, you pay for their drinks for the rest of the night. I was a victim of this prank once, and I accepted, thinking it was an honest offer of a drink. Needless to say, it was my only visit to the Leviathan.

Farlight: The Living Ghost Town.name: 远光：活幽灵（特征）城镇 Farlight: The Living Ghost Town
Farlight: The Living Ghost Town.text:
Farlight

SETTLEMENT 9

  • CN
  • CITY

Ghostly shell of a failed city.

Government Consortium of pirates and smugglers

Population 1,561 (32% kragraks, 25% orpoks, 21% hardriggans, 14% chochori, 8% others)

Languages Common, Terran, Orpok, Jotun, Chochori

Religions the Balance, the Eld

Threats ghosts, haunts, undead, criminals, sea monsters

Free Housing Anyone who is willing to clear out the undead and haunts from a building can find a spacious home for free in Farlight; while it might require a bit of a quest, this replaces the usual price for purchasing a home and can allow powerful adventurers, even poor ones, to own a mansion in Farlight.

Captain Hemlock female g'mayun pirate captain and claimed inheritor of Poppy

Captain Loudbelly male orpok pirate captain and gourmand

Willerm's ghost male vermilion dragon ghost architect, teller of tall tales, and creator of Farlight

Due to the diligence of Kraken's navy, there aren't many pirates—well, at least not of the dangerous kind— within a day's sail of Kraken. But if there were, they'd be in Farlight.

Built over 100 years ago by Willerm, a vermilion dragon civic planner who intended it to be a great metropolis, Farlight is built into the side of a hill. When seen from the harbor, it looks more like a delightful dollhouse town than a living city. White stone buildings gleam in the sun, and picturesque roads and stairways wind up the terraced hillside.

Unfortunately for Willerm, Farlight never grew to be much more than a model of a city (although a full- sized one). Farlight has wide streets, an extensive sewer system, and a colossal lighthouse to usher shipping into the harbor. However, it's missing one vital element for city growth: access to plenty of fresh water. Willerm spent half his fortune building Farlight and the other half building aqueducts over the Emerald Hills to supply water for the town. But he went broke long before the aqueducts were finished. Today, the crumbling aqueducts span half of the Emerald Hills, a monument to failed ambition, and Farlight remains a small town squatting on the foundations of a metropolis.

Today, Farlight is seen as a haunted city: haunted by its past and by the future Willerm dreamed for it; haunted by the spirits of the kragrak workers who died to build it; and haunted by Willerm himself.

But even with its troubled past, Farlight is not a ghost town! Well, it is a ghost town, but they mostly keep to themselves. Those who are stubborn enough or brave enough to live in Farlight claim to live in one of the most unique cities in Alacar. Of course, they must depend on ships to deliver food and water, but nearly everyone lives in a mansion with a breathtaking ocean view!

Inhabited mainly by the descendants of the original population (the workers, primarily kragraks, who built the city and the Rumplank and Kraken residents who flocked to Farlight in the city's first few optimistic months), mix with the sea folk who use Farlight as a convenient harbor. Farlight is little more than a convenient stop on the Kraken-Bluebell trade route and has a light trading scene, but there are two types of people for whom Farlight is a destination rather than a landmark: explorers and pirates.

Explorers, like me, often use Farlight as a base of operations for their journeys in the Buckshot Isles. In that far-flung archipelago, there are many mysteries to unravel and treasures to discover—and between all that unraveling and discovering, a person likes a comfortable bed and a hot bath.

Pirates flock to Farlight for their own reasons: big- city amenities, a lack of a court system, and a uniquely well-defended harbor that even Kraken's Ironworks ships won't brave.

Life in Farlight

Most of Farlight's residents are temporary inhabitants and mainly consist of ship crews docked in the Grand Harbor. Under the watchful eye of the Farlight Lighthouse and the catapults of the Fortress, merchant ships are safe from ransacking pirates, while visiting pirates are secure against reprisals from their past victims.

Shipping in the Grand Harbor ranges from fishing smacks to trade galleons, but pride of place goes to the food and water vessels that supply the town. No skipper is cheered as loudly as the water barge captain, and everyone in town comes aboard to pay their penny and fill their buckets.

Most of Farlight's permanent residents live in the apartments and mansions near Willerm Square. A few of the grander houses further inland are inhabited as well, and other fine houses are there for the taking. In Farlight, everyone can live like a king if they wish! Willerm built houses before there was a housing market, and the demand never met Willerm's supply, with residences of all sizes lying empty. However, most people huddle together near the harbor, forgoing palatial estates in exchange for relative safety from gangs, ghosts, and ghouls.

What's that about ghosts and ghouls, you ask? Well, most cities are home to a spirit or two, but Farlight has more than its fair share of undead. As Willerm saw his funds dwindling while constructing Farlight, he cut quite a few corners. Safety standards were relaxed, and numerous workers died in construction accidents. No one knows quite how many since, hoping to avoid scandal, Willerm falsified records and disposed of corpses in unorthodox ways. Quite a few Farlight houses have a skeleton in the closet, the foundation, or the cistern. However, such secrets can't be hidden forever. Over the years, angry spirits have arisen, a lingering witness to the death toll of Farlight's construction. And as these spirits continue to arise, the undead outnumber the living in many parts of town. However, these spirits mostly keep to the place of their demise and leave the living alone. Mostly.

Of course, laborers are not the only souls that haunt Farlight. The most famous resident of Farlight is the architect himself—the ghost of Willerm himself. The vermilion dragon can often be seen walking the city streets, extolling its virtues as a bustling metropolis and hub of commerce to anyone who will stop to listen. Even in death, Willerm is obsessed with his grandiose vision of Farlight.

People of Farlight

Farlight has no traditionally chartered government. Official duties, such as civic defense and peacekeeping, are handled by a "Consortium of Traders." To put it in plain terms, it's an alliance of pirates and smugglers, along with the merchants who pay them protection money. To my mind, putting pirates in charge of a city is like setting the orpok to guard the feast, but they do keep a limited sort of order within the city's harbor.

While docked in the Grand Harbor, each ship with membership in the Consortium must contribute an officer and some crew to Farlight's constabulary. These constables patrol the harbor district, garrison the fortress, and operate the lighthouse. While they provide some protection to residents, their most important purpose is to guard the harbor against Kraken's pirate-hunting navy.

As ships enter and leave Farlight, the composition of its governing body changes accordingly. One of the most persistent members of the Consortium is the pirate Captain Hemlock (female g'mayun captain). She claims to be Poppy von Barnacle's direct descendant and the rightful inheritor of her riches, although no particular evidence has ever been discovered in support of this claim.

When Captain Hemlock isn't in the harbor or chasing merchant ships, she's sailing among the Buckshot Islands… searching for something. Perhaps she's searching for a relic of Poppy's or maybe just some random treasure, but whatever she's looking for, stay out of her way. If you're among the Buckshots and see her flag, a blue pennon bearing two green feathers, hightail it out of there! She considers every treasure hunter in the Buckshot Isles to be a competitor and a trespasser. If she finds you and you're lucky, she'll merely search your ship; if you're unlucky, she'll sink you out of hand.

Captain Loudbelly (male orpok captain) is another pirate who never seems to stray too far from the harbor. He flies his flag, a skull and crossed forks, aboard the Gastronome, a galleon that can rival any noble's yacht for opulence. Unlike Captain Hemlock, he always treats prisoners with courtly politeness. The feasts he gives to his prisoners almost make it worth being robbed! Captain Loudbelly claims that his personal cook, Jascar Thoo, is the best chef in the Isles, and having sampled the Gastronome's hospitality myself, I find it hard to disagree.

Among Farlight's many spirits, Willerm's ghost (male vermilion dragon ghost architect) is the most famous. Many people have a story of a run-in with Farlight's architect, and many visitors go out of their way to find him. More of a bore than a threat, he can manifest in any part of the city, wherever an audience can be found. His favorite subject is the city itself, and he gushes about the magnificence of whatever architectural feature catches his eye—the sidewalks, the drains, the lighthouse. And since he's undead, he doesn't even have to stop to take a breath! In Willerm's eyes, the city is a vibrant city with a population in the tens of thousands. Attempting to convince him otherwise is neither polite nor safe. Your best course of action is to listen to Willerm for a few minutes and then claim you have a pressing engagement.

Exploring Farlight

The following is a sample of some prominent locations found in Farlight.

Grand Harbor

The widest harbor in Goldcrop, the Grand Harbor, is lined with marble walkways and quays. It could berth a fleet, and while it usually has considerably less traffic than that, it is far from deserted. Pirate galleys, merchant cogs, and even the occasional Kraken frigate lie peacefully alongside each other under the frowning regard of the Fortress. Any ship foolish enough to begin hostilities within Farlight is quickly sunk by artillery… or, worse, attracts the attention of the Lighthouse.

Farlight Lighthouse

Farlight is a wonder among lighthouses. It is the crowning achievement of Willerm's genius and gives its name to the city itself.

Four hundred feet tall, Farlight's beacon can be seen miles away by sea and can even be seen from high vantage points in Kraken and north Bluebell. While most lighthouses keep a signal fire burning throughout the night, Farlight sweeps the waters with a permanent beam of magical sunlight, specially enchanted to shine through clouds and fog.

As with many of Willerm's achievements, the Lighthouse is as much of a curse as a blessing. Its marvelous light has attracted the notice of a flock of muted delvers, malevolent sea monsters that hunt amidst a magical aura of silence. Muted delvers are attracted to magical light the way moths are to flame, and to keep the city safe, the lighthouse must continually shine its beacon upon waters far from the harbor. While sailors use Farlight's signal for navigation, they know to avoid the spotlight on the sea, where monstrous fish creatures sport and play.

However, Farlighters are good at turning curses into advantages—if they weren't, they would have moved long ago. The Consortium of Traders has turned the lighthouse's magical beam and their unwanted guests into one of the city's most feared defenses.

A few years ago, angered by a brazen act of piracy committed by Farlight sailors, Kraken sent a great fleet to conquer the city once and for all. They didn't make it within ten miles of the city. When the lighthouse shone its magical light upon the lead ship, the muted delvers, frenzied by its glow, climbed aboard, devoured every crewmember they could reach, and smashed the ship to timbers. The remaining Kraken vessels fled home and haven't returned to Farlight since.

The Fortress

Invaders who somehow make it past the Lighthouse would have to deal with the Fortress, an island citadel that squats in the center of the crescent-shaped harbor. Its catapults and ballistae can rain destruction on any part of the harbor—and quite a ways inland. Its weapons are crewed by members of the Consortium of Traders, not by the city's permanent residents; fearing threats to their power from all directions, they keep those weapons trained seaward and inland.

Willerm Square

Willerm Square, designed to be a wealthy harborside enclave, is the home of practically all the land-dwelling residents of Farlight. It is the only area of the city that the Consortium of Traders patrols, and it is close to the harbor, the city's source of food and water.

Even the poorest residents of Willerm Square live in grand mansions and apartments with balconies overlooking the sea and the Great Square. The Square itself, an expanse of white cobbles, features an immense, much-defaced statue of Willerm, and is one of the most common haunts of Willerm's Ghost. The rest of the square is home to the city's market and a surprisingly thriving theater scene.

The Sewers

The sewers of Farlight were designed to serve a population of one hundred thousand. With no water supply and barely any population to support, many sections are positively pristine. These conditions have made the sewers an enticing home for criminals, fugitives on the run from the Consortium of Traders, and plenty of ghosts.

One well-traveled corner of the sewer, often visited by locals and tourists, is Tosher's Museum, which is stuffed with oddities and (presumably) stolen and "lost" goods of all kinds. Its proprietor, Tosher, is the island's preeminent fence and claims he can get anything for you (if he doesn't have one already) and will buy anything from you… if the price is low enough.

If you are to visit his shop, don't expect to see Tosher. Instead, his voice echoes through the sewers as he brags and haggles with visitors. Visitors are expected to take only what they paid for and leave exact change. Although no visible guards patrol the museum, everyone knows better than to steal from Tosher. It is said that those who try to cheat Tosher are often seen later in the sewers... as ghosts.

The Palace

Designed for a government that never materialized, the Palace is a marble haven for scoundrels and criminals. Several competing pirate cartels, including Captains Hemlock and Loudbelly, live in different palace wings and are often in states of open war with one another. The lushly carpeted hallways of the Palace are as dangerous as dungeons, and its ballrooms often act as battlefields. Even Consortium captains don't venture into the less-frequented sections of the palace, where chambers oozes, and worse things are said to roam.

Molmist: Humble Homestead.name: 雾蛾：谦卑家园 Molmist: Humble Homestead
Molmist: Humble Homestead.text:
Molmist

Settlement 3

  • N
  • Village

Small village with a nearby dungeon.

Government Mayorship

Population 324 (38% g'mayun, 23% kragraks, 17% hardriggans, 10% orpok, 12% others)

Languages Auran, Common, Jotun, Orpok, Terran

Religions the Balance, the Eld

Threats cattle rustlers, dungeon denizens, hinterlands beasts

Linara's Blessings The cleric Linara has outsized talent for such a small village and is willing to provide needed spells in exchange for services or quests. Travelers to Molmist can receive spellcasting services of up to 6th level divine spells from Linara, though only if they remain in her good graces, and she occasionally sells spell scrolls of divine spells up to 6th level as a way.

Broken Gate female sky dragon commander of the town militia and reluctant mayor

Old Redclay male kragrak gunslinger and farmer

Grunk Brothers hardriggan reavers, herders, and occasional cattle rustlers

Linara female galtzagorri priestess of Tova

While a lot of tour guides wouldn't bother to stop in at a little place like the village of Molmist, I think it's important for you to get a sense of what life is like in a typical Goldcrop village. There are a dozen settlements like this one all along the island's coast. So why am I stopping in Molmist specifically, as opposed to one of those other villages? Simple: it's my home town! I can give you an in-depth tour of Molmist that no one else can. Besides, you'll love my Granny Mayfeather's seed cookies.

Even before the days of Poppy von Barnacle, Molmist was probably around the size it is now, and many of the same families have been dwelling here all that time. Some of the more staid families consider even the village's orpoks newcomers, even though they arrived around the time that orpoks first emigrated to the island!

With a history older than the most ancient written history that still remains on the Indigo Isles, Molmist's origins are long forgotten, and they've become shrouded in the mists of time.

In fact, no one has any idea what the village's name means. Some folks think its name originally arose from a corruption of the expression "morning mist," the haze over the western sea that usually burns off shortly after dawn. Others believe it must be a family name, though there's no record of a Molmist family in town. If there's an answer to this question, I bet it lies in the Tunnels— the lava caves that were the first habitations of ancient Molmistians.

Life in Molmist

Molmist is typical of the small coastal villages that dot the Indigo Isles. Its population is split between farmers, fishers, hunters, and herders. This diversity of food sources means that even in a bad year for crops, fishing and livestock can carry it through, and vice versa. The many heritages that make up Molmist are similarly diverse, with no one group predominating, though g'mayun have the highest population in Molmist, as is much the case in Rumplank and across Goldcrop Isle as a whole.

While Molmist trades a bit with Rumplank, it's self-sufficient and fairly isolated; nearly all such trade takes place by sea, as the journey across the jungle and salt desert is too long and deadly for ordinary traders. Molmist doesn't get a lot of strangers, so don't be surprised when you're the center of attention wherever you go. And if you carry weapons or sling spells, I've found that you get even more attention—suspicion from the militia and admiration from younger folk who are interested in the adventurous lifestyle.

People of Molmist

If Molmist is a quiet village, a lot of the credit for that goes to Broken Gate (female sky dragon champion), the commander of the town's tiny militia, and, as of the last election, Molmist's reluctant mayor. Most of Broken Gate's official duties consist of finding lost animals, running off the Grunk Brothers when they get rowdy, and keeping her overenthusiastic deputy Liss the wildfire leshy from burning down the town. Beyond her work, Broken Gate doesn't have much of a personal life. My Granny Mayfeather keeps telling me that I should ask her out on a date, but then my granny is an inveterate matchmaker. Personally, I don't think Broken Gate will ever fall for someone less duty-bound and idealistic than herself.

Old Redclay (male kragrak gunslinger) is one of Molmist's oldest farmers. If you walk out of the village to the south, you'll probably see him standing in the middle of one of his fields with a musket over his shoulder, looking a bit like a rocky scarecrow. He's Broken Gate's most trusted militia deputy, both for his keen people sense and his crack shooting.

The six Grunk Brothers (male hardriggan reavers) are the closest thing Molmist has to a crime problem. The Grunks are thyalodon herders who live in the North Hills. When they're at home, they brawl with each other; when they come into town, they brawl with everyone else. And if they happen to come across anyone else's herds along the way, they're not above a little cattle rustling.

Exploring Molmist

The following is a sample of some of the prominent locations found in Molmist.

The Slide

The Slide is the first cave I ever explored. Ah, memories! I knew then that I wanted to be an adventurer as I quietly made my way through the tunnels, keeping an eye out for dangerous animals and collecting odd objects left behind by previous cave dwellers. The Slide is one of a half-dozen lava caves within a day's travel of Molmist, collectively called the Tunnels. Most people leave the Tunnels alone since they tend to house dangerous animals and even the occasional bandit. From my explorations, I think that the Tunnels were inhabited long before Molmist existed as a village. There I've found clay pot shards, stone and bronze arrowheads, and even small pieces of amber and coral jewelry.

The Slide, so-called for the precipitous slope of its entry tunnel, is typical of Molmist's lava caves. Its tube- shaped passages lead to arched caverns—all once filled with flowing lava during this cave's creation. Tree roots hang from the ceiling in some areas of the cave, and in other places, volcanic forces have melted the ceiling and walls into lovely whorls and spirals. When I explored the Slide, I saw nothing more ferocious than a handful of toogtoogs, but I've heard that more dangerous creatures have moved in since.

The Dock

Like most villages on Goldcrop, Molmist's hub is a town square adjoining its dock. Besides being the center of the fishing trade, the Dock is home to the village's major businesses—Gundry's Sundries, the Tap, the Redfeather Armory, and the Brimming Beaker.

Around sunset, Simarr (female xotlxotl bard) usually perches on one of the dock's pilings and plays gentle ballads on her harp. As townsfolk finish their days' work, they gather at the Dock to gossip, eat dinner at one of the Tap's outside benches, or just relax and listen to the music.

Gundry's Sundries

If you're looking for anything at all in Molmist, from adventuring supplies to a 20-pound bag of brown sugar, Gundry's should be your first stop. Gundry (nonbinary bannog merchant) doesn't talk much, but they'll point you to what you're looking for without getting up from the pile of feed bags they use as a chair. In fact, pretty much the only time Gundry moves a muscle is when you bring them a magic item to look at. They're an expert at identifying magic, and they'll do it for free. To this day, I haven't gotten Gundry to open up about their past, but I'm sure it was an adventurous one.

Shrine of Balance

The Shrine of Balance, which is maintained by Linara (female galtzagorri cleric), a priestess of Tova, serves as the place of worship for all Molmist followers of the deities of Balance. After all, the village isn't big enough to house temples for each and every deity. Instead, the shrine is lined with humble statues of the gods, with each of the icons accompanied by a small offering bowl. No matter which bowl you choose for your donation, however, your offering goes to the same place in the end. Every day, long after the sun sinks beneath the horizon and just before closing up the shrine for the night, Linara collects the contents of the bowls. She then divides the total proceeds among the most needy members of the community, minus a small amount for the shrine's maintenance.

Hey, can I trust you with a secret? Linara may not look like much more than the pocket-sized priestess of a dusty shrine in an isolated little village. But remember, in life, just as in adventures, appearances can be deceiving. In fact, there's much more to Linara than she lets on. In fact, if there's a more blessed cleric on Goldcrop, I certainly don't know about them. She tries to keep it quiet, but the rumor in Molmist is that she was once a high priestess at the Rumplank cathedral, outranking even Prismatic Colvi himself, before she abruptly quit and buried herself in this backwater village. Most people in Molmist don't know about her history, though, so please do me a favor and don't spread it around, alright?

Linara keeps it quiet, but she can cure a lot more ailments than most shrine tenders. If you come down with a case of something serious— like death, for instance—Linara can see you right away, and don't worry if you can't afford the usual fees. She also accepts payment for shrine duties and good deeds.

Elder Grove

Just outside of town, the Elder Grove is a place sacred to the Eld religion. There you can often find Molmist's two Eld philosophers, Ambis (male rhizo bacoo ranger) and Sooth (hypisk chochori monk).

With an appearance resembling an active little tree stump, Ambis stomps around the forest, hunting for game and culling the area's more dangerous monsters. Ambis may be a chatterbox, but each of his words is worth its weight in gold to a fellow adventurer like me. If you're looking to find—or avoid—a particular beast, talk to Ambis. He knows the signs and habitats of every local monster.

Sooth could not be less like Ambis. While Ambis is never still, Sooth hardly ever moves. Ambis tells me that while they appear to be sleeping or sitting motionless, their minds are engaged in elemental meditations beyond either of our comprehensions. If sitting still truly furthers their studies, they must be close to a grand master by now.

The Tap

The Tap is Molmost's inn and tavern. Garkley (male g'mayun innkeeper), the curmudgeonly proprietor, is an excellent cook who doesn't overcharge for his food. Add in the fact that he's a craggily handsome specimen of g'mayunhood, and it's no surprise that dinner at the Tap is usually a well-attended affair. If you're looking to stay the night at the Tap, you'll find rooms that are comfortable, if not particularly clean. Besides cooking, bartending, and managing the stable, Garkley does all the cleaning himself. It's too much work for one person, but Garkley says he's just too busy to find a good assistant. There are, of course, rumors of a tragic past and a broken heart, though Garkley maintains that the right partner just hasn't come along yet.

Redfeather Armory

The sign outside Redfeather Armory shows a brawny g'mayun wielding a hammer, so most people are surprised to enter the smithy and find a wildfire leshy at the anvil. Liss (genderfluid wildfire leshy smith), while not a g'mayun, is a patriotic supporter of the Redfeather royal family (as they will tell you at length if you're not careful). If you're equally enthusiastic about Rumplank's g'mayun royals, you're likely to get a discount on your next big-ticket purchase (though not too big a discount! Liss is still working off a substantial debt after they ran too hot and burned down their own smithy... twice.)

In addition to being a semi-successful smith, Liss is also a deputy member of the town watch, serving under the watchful eye of Commander Broken Gate. When called upon to defend the village, Liss is a sturdy fighter. Even when not called upon, they're unfortunately rather overenthusiastic about the enforcement of the law. If you don't mind, we won't go into the smithy on our tour. There's just a tiny chance I may have some back taxes due.

Brimming Beaker

The Brimming Beaker sounds like the name of a tavern, but I wouldn't encourage you to drink its brews. Clerihew Guzzle (male orpok alchemist), as well as a potionmaster, is an alchemical dabbler.

While his shelves are stocked with the minor elixirs of life and lesser antidotes that pay his bills day to day, most of Clerihew's experiments are in the alcoholic line. That's because he longs to achieve culinary immortality by giving his name to a beverage of his own invention. His most successful concoction so far, Guzzle Sherry, was meant to be an after-dinner aperitif but is more commonly used as a slightly cheaper alternative to alchemist's fire.

Private Homes

Molmist is home to some 300 folks, most of whom live in the forty-odd huts and cottages surrounding the Docks. Why, if any of you are from Molmist or decide to move here, you might have a family dwelling here yourself!

When I'm in Molmist, I always stay in the Tixima ancestral cottage with my grandmother, Granny Mayfeather (female g'mayun witch). If you need a curse examined that you just can't bring yourself to explain to Linara at the Shrine of Balance, or if you just need a place to stay, please come visit. Granny is always ready with sensible advice and good g'mayun home cooking, and she's not a bad hand at cards. (I've been playing her for 20 years, and I haven't won a game of Poppy's Prize yet!)

Molmist As A Home Base

Whether the characters are about to embark on the Jewel of the Indigo Isles adventure or on another campaign set on the islands, Molmist makes a good choice for a hometown. It's got a selection of useful shops and a small dungeon ready to be populated with monsters. And its NPCs also demonstrate the range of new ancestries and themes available in the Indigo Isles. Consider giving each character from Molmist a family member, friend, mentor, or trainer based on the character's ancestry or class:

Ambis: bacoo, druid, barbarian, or ranger characters, or characters interested in monster parts

Clerihew: orpok, alchemist, or inventor characters

Granny Mayfeather: g'mayun, sorcerer, summoner, witch, or wizard characters

The Grunk Brothers: hardriggan or rogue characters

Linara: galtzigorri, cleric, or oracle characters

Liss: leshy, fighter, or magus characters

Old Redclay: kragrak or gunslinger characters

Broken Gate: dragon, champion, or investigator characters

Simarr: xotlxotl, bard, or eldamon trainer characters

Sooth: chochori, elemental avatar, monk, or psychic characters

Even if characters aren't from Molmist, they might find it worth a visit. Molmist is a sleepy village, and the greater events of the world pass it by. Even when epic battles rock Goldcrop, they reach Molmist only as vague gossip. It's a safe place for the characters to rest and recuperate, with an excellent healer who can cast high-level spells, including raise dead.

History.name: 历史 History
History.text:
让我们先从一堂历史课开始，好吗？你当然可以直接跳到好玩的部分——酒馆、派对之类的——但我觉得你最好还是先了解背景，嗯？朗普兰克的历史与其庇护海盗的传奇——波比·冯·藤壶——密不可分。想快速了解这段故事，你可以前往桶广场，当地雕塑家阿比代尔·奥伦斯沃斯最著名的作品——一尊采用对立式站姿、通体鎏金的瓷质波比雕像，简称“鎏金海盗”——就矗立在广场西端。这尊等身雕像的基座上，一块铜牌铭文简要概述了朗普兰克的建城故事：“鎏金海盗在辉煌的私掠、寻宝与解放俘虏生涯之后，于金作物岛屿定居。她的诸多壮举包括酿造出金作物举世闻名的金色朗姆酒、发现希萨玛雅的失落宝库，并从暴虐的‘至高海王’手中解放了金币与船员。当她退隐至宁静的朗普兰克小镇时，她慷慨投入的财富与武力促成了村落的爆炸式发展。如今，朗普兰克是靛蓝群岛最大的城市，也是群岛最具传奇色彩的停泊港。朗普兰克以这尊朴素的雕像，纪念其奠基者的慷慨与她那辉煌的侠义生涯。”铭文最下方是朗普兰克的官方格言，献给城市的奠基者：“愿派对永不落幕，波比·冯·藤壶！”

然而，大多数历史学家都同意：在波比到来之前，名为朗普兰克的海滨小村其实就已存在——尽管他们也承认，你仍可以把波比视为同名大城的奠基者，因为这座大城正是在那座旧村之上建成的。哪天你可以去问问历史学家蒙巴克对这场争论的看法，也许边喝几杯边聊！靛蓝群岛历史学会的人似乎怎么也吵不够，他们总爱争论围绕波比传奇的各种细节，连“奠基者”一词究竟意味着什么都能吵起来。这不合我的朗姆口味，但也许合你的？

总之，我说到哪了？在朗普兰克那个小村里，村民们被波比英勇专长与惊险冒险的故事深深吸引。随着时代推移，“鎏金海盗”的名声日益高涨，朗普兰克也随之扩张；本地居民与波比的船员相互交融，并将他们那热情、无忧无虑的生活方式传给后世。波比用她的船只“虹彩女王号”上运来的财富，开辟了朗普兰克与岛屿周边大城镇之间的贸易，而朗普兰克很快在体型与经济产出上都超越了它们。凭借她在战术与组织上的天赋，再加上她那些著名伙伴的协助——包括加尔察戈里法师红袍阿拉霍斯科、克拉格拉克战士石沟的温达约、靛蓝巨龙牧师皮诺梅里·埃莱马特以及哈德里甘游侠疾箭杰西娅——她协助制定了朗普兰克的第一部体质与其沿用至今的君主制政治体系。她在许多著名的朗普兰克事件中都起了关键作用，包括玫瑰水战斗、七年沉默、科洛拉娜以及凋萎之冬。她对这座城市的影响惊人而至关重要，推动朗普兰克从默默无闻中崛起，因此波比·冯·藤壶居住在朗普兰克的时代常被称为“朗普兰克文艺复兴”。文艺复兴意为重生，而朗普兰克重生为靛蓝群岛最具影响力的力量。

自波比逝去之后的时代里，朗普兰克依旧繁荣兴盛。我想，这座城市的昌盛大概源于一种独特的结合：来自最早居民、代代相传的昂扬之心，以及波比与其船员带来的务实物质财富与知识——但每个人都有自己的理论。许多造访朗普兰克的人并不知道波比·冯·藤壶的故事，也不了解她对城市贡献的细节；但所有人都明白，“色彩之城”对其庇护奠基者负有深深的感恩之债。

Let's start with a history lesson, shall we? You can skip to the fun part, the taverns and parties and such, but I say you're better off with the context, eh? The history of Rumplank is inextricably intertwined with the legend of its patron pirate, Poppy von Barnacle. One can get a brief synopsis of the story by heading to Barrel Square, where the local sculptor Abidale Ollensworth's most famous creation—a gilded porcelain statue of Poppy in contrapposto called simply, The Gilded Pirate—stands at its western end. At the base of the life-sized statue, an inscription on a copper plaque gives a brief summary of the story of Rumplank's founding: "The Gilded Pirate settled on Goldcrop Island after a dazzling career of freebooting, treasure hunting, and liberating captives. Her many exploits include the creation of Goldcrop's famous golden rum, the discovery of the Lost Trove of Hisamaya, and the liberation of both coin and crew from the tyrannical Sovereign Sea King. When she retired to the sleepy town of Rumplank, her generous investments of fortune and muscle made possible the village's explosive growth. Now, Rumplank is the largest city in the Indigo Isles and the archipelago's most legendary port of call. Rumplank honors the generosity of its founder and her illustrious career of derring-do with this humble statue." At the bottom of the inscription is the official Rumplank motto, addressed to the city's founder: "May the party never end, Poppy von Barnacle!"

Most historians agree, however, that a tiny beachside hamlet named Rumplank actually predated Poppy's arrival, though they'd admit that you could still consider Poppy the founder of the sizable city of the same name, built out of that old hamlet. Ask the historian Monbak about that debate some time, maybe over a few drinks! The folks in the Indigo Isles Historical Society can't seem to get enough of arguing about the specifics of the details surrounding Poppy's legend, including the meaning of the word "founder." Not my cup of rum, but maybe it's yours?

Anyway, where was I? In that hamlet of Rumplank, villagers were enthralled by the stories of Poppy's feats of bravery and harrowing adventures. As time went on and the Gilded Pirate's reputation grew, so did the town of Rumplank, whose native inhabitants intermingled with Poppy's crew and passed on their exuberant, carefree lifestyle to future generations. Poppy used the wealth carried aboard her vessel, the Chromatic Queen, to open up trade between Rumplank and the larger towns around the island, towns which Rumplank soon surpassed in size and economic output. With her knack for tactics and organization, and the assistance of her famous companions, including the galtzagorri mage Arahosko the Red, the kragrak warrior Undayo of Stonegulch, the indigo dragon cleric Pinomeri Elemarte, and the hardriggan ranger Jesea Swiftarrow, she helped establish Rumplank's first constitution and the monarchical political system it still uses to this day. She was pivotal in many famous Rumplank events, including the Battle of Rosewater, the Seven Years of Silence, Colorana, and the Wilted Winter. Her influence on the city was so incredible and vital to its emergence from obscurity that the time of Poppy von Barnacle's residence in Rumplank is often called the Rumplank Renaissance. Renaissance means rebirth, and Rumplank was reborn as the most influential power in the Indigo Isles.

In the time since Poppy's passing, Rumplank has continued to flourish. I think the city's prosperity is probably a result of the unique combination of the buoyant heart passed down from its earliest inhabitants and the practical material wealth and knowledge imported by Poppy and her crew, but everyone has their own theories. Many who visit Rumplank don't know the story of Poppy von Barnacle or the specifics of her contributions to the city, but all understand that the City of Color owes a great debt of gratitude to its patron founder.

Culture.name: 文化 Culture
Culture.text:
伦普兰克独特的文化源于我们的地理特性、强烈的宝藏狩猎根源，以及我们居民的多样性——主要是情感带电的g'mayuns（比如在下），不过也很好地混合了勤劳却随和的orpoks以及许多其他族群。尽管这里最出名的是派对圣地（而且狂欢确实是伦普兰克文化中极为重要的一部分），但这座色彩之城并不只有恣意的酒神狂欢。不，真的……我寄望城！友谊、自由与美食都是伦普兰克人日常生活中的常态，而这些价值往往也按这个顺序被尊崇。谈论伦普兰克人，就是在谈论一群珍视他们的伙伴、为掌握自己命运的权利而战、并会抽出time来享受人生精致之物的人民。致我那些orpok读者：请别担心我把美食放在第三位；我想我们都同意，友谊与自由更为重要，不是吗？

Rumplank's unique culture is a result of our geographic peculiarities, strong tradition of treasure hunting, and the diversity of our populace—primarily emotionally charged g'mayuns (like yours truly), though with a good mix of industrious yet easygoing orpoks and many others. Although it's known best as a party mecca (and although partying is a very large part of Rumplank culture), there's more to the City of Color than wanton bacchanalia. No, really… I promise! Friends, freedom, and food are all fixtures of Rumplankers' daily lives, values which are often upheld in that order. To speak of Rumplankers is to speak of a people who cherish their companions, fight for the right to chart their own destinies, and take time to enjoy the finer things in life. To my orpok readers, please don't fret that I placed food in third place; I think we can all agree that friends and freedom are yet more important, no?

Expressions And Aphorisms.name: 俗语与格言 Expressions And Aphorisms
Expressions And Aphorisms.text:
朗普兰克人使用许多常见说法，这些说法与金作物岛屿的独特特性、朗普兰克的住民或这座城市的历史有关。以下只是你可能在五彩之城街头听到的几句俗语。

  • “愿派对永不结束！”一种普遍的喜悦或赞同表达。朗普兰克的官方格言。
  • “羽飞之处，敌人沉没！”大意是朗普兰克人——尤其是格梅尤恩——在他人失败时仍能坚持不懈。
  • “一船朋友成一艘船。”指一群相处融洽的盟友能成就大事。
  • “红羽星期二”指一顿丰盛大餐或奢华派对，例如“那场节庆真是地道的红羽星期二！”
  • “浮扬之心”指朗普兰克最具代表性的慷慨与好心情的灵体。
  • “镀金的烂摊子”用来暗示事情或许没起初看上去那么糟，或指一种夹杂好坏的福祸相依，例如“有条大蛇刚搬进了我的地窖，但至少它解决了血鼠泛滥。真是个镀金的烂摊子！”)
  • “水清则当恐惧。水粉则宜饮！”一句古老说法，如今最常用于鼓励畅饮。如果你非要知道，靛青群岛历史学会的蒙巴克声称，这句俗语的根源可追溯到朗普兰克的前罂粟时代——在罂粟的船员安装能够正确过滤地下水的水井之前。那时候，或如蒙巴克所推测，朗普兰克人净化水的最佳手段就是把水与葡萄酒或烈酒混合。因此，“清水”指尚未处理的水，而“粉水”则被认为安全，因为它已用红葡萄酒净化过。
  • “永不落幕的盛会”指朗普兰克的派对式生活方式，或泛指生活本身。

Rumplankers use a number of common expressions with ties to the unique features of Goldcrop Island, Rumplank’s occupants, or the city’s history. The following are just a few sayings one might hear on the streets of the City of Color.

  • “May the party never end!” A general expression of joy or approval. The official motto of Rumplank.
  • “Feathers fly where foes founder!” Roughly translates to Rumplankers, but g’mayuns especially, persevere when others fail.
  • “A boatload of friends makes a ship.” Refers to a group of allies who get along can accomplish great things.
  • “Redfeather Tuesday” refers to a sumptuous feast or extravagant party, as in “The festival was a right Redfeather Tuesday!”
  • “Buoyant heart” refers to Rumplank’s quintessential spirit of generosity and good cheer.
  • “Gilded mess” is used to suggest that perhaps matters aren't as bad as they initially seem, or to refer to a mixed blessing, as in “A big snake’s just moved into my cellar, but at least it's taken care of the blood mouse infestation. What a gilded mess!”)
  • “Water clear, right to fear. Water pink, good to drink!” an old expression now used most commonly to encourage indulgence in drink. If you must know, Monbak of the Indigo Isles Historical Society claims that the saying’s roots can be traced to Rumplank’s pre-Poppy past, before Poppy's crew installed wells capable of properly filtering groundwater. Back then, or so Monbak proposes, Rumplankers’ best means of purifying water would have been by mixing it with wine or spirits. Thus, “clear water” was water that hadn’t yet been treated, whereas “pink water” was presumed safe because it had been purified with red wine.
  • “The never-ending gala” is the Rumplank party lifestyle, or life in general.

Food And Drink.name: 食物与饮料 Food And Drink
Food And Drink.text:
如果说大多数人对朗普兰克（Rumplank）只知道一件事，那就是镇民热爱吃喝。也因此，我们当中许多人把烹饪、酿造和蒸馏视为代代相传的古老根源；在许多家庭里，学会烹制一道颇为棘手的家传食谱，是从一代传到下一代的成人礼。朗普兰克几乎家家都有自己的厨房，或有许可使用共用的户外烹饪区域；一天中任何时刻，你都能从那里闻到刚切开的水果清香、滋滋作响的油脂气味，以及咸香的烤蔬菜香。的确，多数朗普兰克人更像是整天不停“打牙祭”的人，而非严格遵守用餐时间的大吃大喝者；这种特性自然而然地促成了（又或许正是由此而来）金穗岛（Goldcrop Island）著名的慢炸帕亚约斯（payayos）习俗——这是一种可热食亦可冷食、可鲜食亦可腌藏的手指小吃，种类几近无穷。

传统的朗普兰克餐食包含多种金穗岛（Goldcrop Island）原生的主食，包括油脂丰厚的侏儒椰子、多汁的粉红黑麦瓜、香甜的白色菠萝，以及一种形状奇特、味苦的块茎“鲁巴洛（rubalo）”。后者在金穗本地人中颇受欢迎，但对几乎所有外来者而言都属于“越吃越习惯”的口味。雨季时，鲁巴洛派是经典的朗普兰克慰藉食物，也常常用来招待孩子和体弱多病的人。外来者发现朗普兰克在干燥天气里的传统菜肴更容易入口；据说在炎热的金穗日子里，没有什么比一片菠萝椰子奶油蛋糕更顺口。尽管金穗岛（Goldcrop Island）上还有各式各样其他可食用的生物，包括塞亚罗顿（thyalodons），朗普兰克人主要从昆虫与海产中获取蛋白质——这两者在岛屿及其周边都极为丰富。最早抵达朗普兰克的奥珀克（orpoks）带来了一本厚厚的丰盛秋葵浓汤、清汤与炖煮食谱目录；在随后的数百年里，这些食谱不断改良，加入了本地食材，例如爽脆的丛林螳螂、酸香的山地鲶鱼、甜美的夏季月虾，以及油润的萨里-萨尼鱿鱼。至于陆上食材，许多朗普兰克的格梅云（g'mayun）居民会把啮齿动物——包括本地的血鼠——当作日常食性的一部分。在朗普兰克，看到人们当街抓起老鼠或小鼠生吃并不罕见；这种景象可能会让大陆来客感到不适——他们通常把这类害兽与瘟疫或疫病联系在一起——但对朗普兰克人来说这不算什么。事实上，“找到一只鼠，就吃一只鼠”的做法，让朗普兰克的啮齿动物数量维持在远比你想象中更合理的水平，尤其考虑到这座城市的体量。

If there's one thing most people know about Rumplank, it's that the townsfolk love to eat and drink. Naturally, then, many of us view cooking, brewing, and distilling as time-honored traditions, and in many households learning how to cook a tricky family recipe is a rite of passage passed down from one generation to the next. Nearly every home in Rumplank has its own personal kitchen or access to a shared outdoor cooking area, from which one can smell fresh-cut fruit, sizzling grease, and savory roasting vegetables at any hour of the day. Indeed, most Rumplankers are day-long grazers rather than gorgers who adhere to strict mealtimes, a trait which naturally led to (or perhaps resulted from) Goldcrop Island's celebrated custom of slow-fried payayos—finger foods served hot and cold, fresh or preserved, and of which there are a nearly infinite variety.

Traditional Rumplank fare includes a variety of staples native to Goldcrop Island, including fatty dwarf coconuts, juicy pink rye-melons, sweet white pineapples, and a peculiar-shaped bitter tuber called rubalo, the latter of which is popular among Goldcrop natives but regarded as an acquired taste for nearly everyone else. In the rainy season, rubalo pie is a classic Rumplank comfort food, and it's often served to children and sickly people as well. Non-natives find Rumplank's traditional dry-weather dishes much easier to enjoy; it's said that nothing goes down smoother on a hot Goldcrop day than a slice of pineapple coconut cream cake. While there are a variety of other edible creatures on Goldcrop Island, including thyalodons, Rumplankers derive most of their protein from insects and seafood, both of which abound on and around the island. The first orpoks to arrive at Rumplank brought with them a thick catalog of hearty gumbo, broth, and stew recipes which have, in the intervening centuries, been modified to incorporate local ingredients such as crunchy jungle mantids, tangy mountain catfish, sweet summer moon-shrimp, and oily sali-sani squid. Regarding terrestrial fare, many g'mayun Rumplankers enjoy rodents, including native blood mice, as part of their regular diet. In Rumplank, it's not uncommon to see folks snatch up and eat rats or mice raw, right off the street; such a sight may disturb continentalists who typically associate such vermin with plague or pestilence, but to Rumplankers it's no big deal. In fact, the practice of "find a mouse, eat a mouse" keeps Rumplank's rodent populations at a far more reasonable level than one might expect for a city of its size.

Holidays And Festivals.name: 节日与庆典 Holidays And Festivals
Holidays And Festivals.text:
节日这一概念对我们这些朗普兰克人来说多少有些陌生，因为我们把每一天都当作假日，把每一口新鲜空气的呼吸都当作庆祝的理由。若真有人费心去维持那种记录时代的器具，狂欢节、纪念庆典与各类纪念日足以轻易填满朗普兰克人的日历。相反，大多数市民都乐于从一个社交场合稀里糊涂地晃到另一个，几乎不讲礼数也不顾时辰。即便是在朗普兰克短暂停留的访客，也会发现这座城市有某种特别之处，这种效果会诱发一种自然而然的飘然陶醉，无需酒精或改变心智的物质（尽管许多朗普兰克人也同样享受这些）。金穗岛屿的老住民把这种特殊的欢腾与好心情称作“永不落幕的盛典”或“轻盈之心”。说某人拥有轻盈之心，意思是他们无忧无虑，决定将整颗心奉献给他人，并以放下忧虑来交换生命的诸多喜悦。
